Illustrated Sample Chapter
Chapter 4

Book2: The Day I Could Not See My Own Slides

I stood in front of my own customers in Thailand and could not read the words on my own projector screen. That was the day I knew something had to change. — From my notes, written soon after

Bangkok, A Product Lecture

I was fifty years old and standing at the front of a meeting room in our Thailand office. It was a working day. I had flown in to give a product presentation to a group of customers and partners. The slides were already on the projector. The lights were dimmed. The audience was settled. I had given this kind of lecture many times before.

I turned to look at my own screen on the wall.

The words were blurred. Not a little blurred — completely blurred. I could see the colours and the shapes and the rough outline of the slide, but the actual text was a soft smear. I could not read a single line.

This had never happened to me before. Not in any city, not in any meeting room, not in any year of my life.

I gave the lecture from memory. I knew the material well enough that the audience did not notice anything was wrong. But I noticed. For the entire hour I was speaking, one part of me was talking about our product, and another part was quietly asking, what just happened to my eyes?

When the lecture was over and the customers had left, I sat down in the empty meeting room. The slides were still on the screen. I walked closer. Closer. Closer still. The text only became readable when I was almost near enough to touch the wall.

I had a problem.

The Shopping Mall and the Glasses That Would Not Fit

The next day, I went to a large shopping mall in Bangkok to find glasses. I assumed this would be a simple matter. I would walk into an optical shop. The optometrist would test my eyes. They would hand me a pair of glasses. The problem would be solved.

It was not simple.

I sat in the chair while the optometrist worked through the usual lenses. Better? Or worse? Better? Or worse? Each combination he tried felt almost right but never quite right. One eye would feel comfortable, the other would feel pulled. Or the distance would clear but the words on the test card would seem to tilt. He frowned, changed lenses, tried again.

After more than an hour, he admitted he could not find a suitable prescription for me. He suggested I try a different shop.

So I tried a different shop. The same thing happened. Then a third shop. The same again.

By the end of the day I had visited three optical stores in the same mall. Three optometrists, three sets of lenses, three apologies. None of them could put together a pair of glasses that felt right for both of my eyes at once.

And there was a second problem, even more important than the first. When I did wear the trial lenses — even for short periods — I felt very unwell. Within about an hour, my head began to ache. The ache spread from behind my eyes to the back of my skull. When I tried to walk afterwards, I felt as if I might fall down. The floor seemed to tilt. My balance was disturbed in a way I had never experienced before.

The glasses, even the closest approximation they could find, were giving me headaches and making me unsteady on my feet.

I left the mall that evening empty-handed and a little frightened.

Three Days of Searching

That night, back in my hotel room, I made a decision. If conventional glasses would not work for me — and they would not — then I would have to find another way. There had to be another way. I refused to accept that my working life was over because of my eyes.

I spent the next three days searching. I read everything I could find about vision problems in middle age. I read about presbyopia. I read about myopia. I read about astigmatism and dry eyes and computer strain. I read articles by ophthalmologists and articles by people who distrusted ophthalmologists.

Somewhere in the middle of those three days, I came across the name of an American eye doctor I had never heard of before. Dr. William Horatio Bates. A book he had written more than a hundred years ago, called Perfect Sight Without Glasses.

I read everything I could find about his methods. They were simple. They were free. They asked nothing of me except time and patience.

They were also dismissed by most modern ophthalmologists as outdated. I noted that. I also noted that I had just visited three modern ophthalmologists who could not help me, and whose lenses had given me headaches and made me feel as if I might fall.

I decided to give Dr. Bates one month.

Six O'Clock at the Swimming Pool

I needed a place to practice. I needed to be away from my desk, away from the screens that had hurt my eyes in the first place, away from any temptation to cut the session short and return to work.

The swimming pool of my apartment building opened at six o'clock in the morning. At six o'clock there was almost nobody there. The sun was rising. The air was cool and clean. The water was still.

I decided that this would be my place.

Every morning for the next thirty days, I went to the pool from six to seven. I did not always swim. The pool was simply my quiet room with a sky above it.

My one-hour morning practice settled, over the first week, into something like this:

TimeWhat I DidWhy It Mattered
6:00 – 6:10 Sunning. Standing at the edge of the pool with eyes closed, face turned gently toward the rising sun, slowly turning my head from side to side. The early morning sun is soft and safe. Light through closed eyelids calms and nourishes the eyes.
6:10 – 6:25 Palming. Sitting on a poolside chair, warm palms cupped over closed eyes, breathing slowly, imagining a deep soft black. The deepest rest the eyes can receive. The foundation of the whole practice.
6:25 – 6:35 Swinging. Standing with feet apart, gently swaying the whole body from side to side, letting the pool, the sky, and the trees drift past my vision. Releases the tight grip the eyes have learned to keep on the world. Loosens the neck and shoulders too.
6:35 – 6:50 Distance shifting. Looking at the tiles at my feet, then the far end of the pool, then the trees beyond the wall, then back to my feet. Soft, unhurried, no straining. Wakes up the focusing muscles. The most important exercise for both presbyopia and myopia.
6:50 – 7:00 A short, gentle swim or simply sitting with my feet in the water, eyes resting on the horizon. A reward. A way to close the practice peacefully and carry the calm into the workday.

That was my routine. One hour. Five segments. Every single morning for thirty days, without exception.

Why I Did Not Quit

The first week was the hardest. The exercises felt strange. I was a businessman in his fifties, standing by a swimming pool at sunrise, covering my own eyes with my hands, swaying gently from side to side. If a colleague had seen me, I would have been embarrassed.

But I had three reasons to continue, and each was stronger than my embarrassment.

  1. The glasses had failed. Three optometrists had tried and failed. I had nowhere else to turn.
  2. The headaches had warned me. My body had told me, clearly and physically, that lenses were not a safe answer for me. I had to listen.
  3. The exercises were costing me nothing. No money. No risk. Only one hour of a morning that I would otherwise have spent rushing toward a day of more screens.

So I kept going. Day six. Day ten. Day fifteen. Day twenty.

And one morning, somewhere in the fourth week, I looked across the pool and saw the leaves on a tree at the far end clearly for the first time in many months. Not a green blur. Individual leaves.

I stood there at the edge of the pool, sixty minutes into my morning practice, and I knew.

Full Recovery

By the end of the thirty days, my eyes had fully recovered. I could read a projector screen from the back of a meeting room. I could read the small print on a contract. I could see road signs at dusk. I could walk into any shop and read the price tags without leaning forward.

I never bought the glasses. I never returned to any of those three optical shops. The headaches I had feared would become a permanent part of my middle age never returned.

I am seventy years old now. Twenty years have passed since that month in Thailand. I have never needed glasses since. I still travel. I still give lectures. I still read the small print. I still see my own slides.

The four exercises I learned from Dr. Bates, practiced at a swimming pool in Bangkok from six to seven every morning for one month, gave me back my eyes.

❦ ❦ ❦

What You Can Take from This Chapter

  • If conventional glasses do not work for you, that is not the end of the story. It may even be the beginning of a better one.
  • If glasses give you headaches or make you feel unsteady, your body is telling you something important. Do not ignore it.
  • You do not need expensive equipment, a special teacher, or a clinic. You need a quiet place, an early hour, and the willingness to stay with a simple practice.
  • One hour a day, every day, for thirty days, is the price. The reward — in my case — was twenty years and counting of clear, glasses-free sight.

The chapters that follow will walk you through each of the four exercises in detail, so that you can build your own one-hour practice — at a pool, in a garden, by a window, or anywhere you can be quiet for an hour of the day.

End of Illustrated Sample Chapter
